name: Korean Air Lines 007
description: Korean Air Lines Flight 007 (KAL007 or KE007) was a scheduled Korean Air Lines flight from New York City to Seoul via Anchorage, Alaska, that was shot down by Soviet fighter jets on August 31, 1983 (Korean time), over Moneron Island, near Sakhalin Island in the Sea of Japan. All 269 passengers and crew aboard were killed, including 75 Koreans. The event sparked international outrage and a diplomatic crisis. Conspiracy theories surrounding the incident persist to this day, particularly within Korean society, fueled by lingering distrust of both the Soviet Union (and later Russia) and, to a lesser extent, the United States. Some theories suggest the flight was on a secret intelligence mission, either knowingly or unknowingly, for the US or South Korea, potentially involving reconnaissance or provocation of Soviet defenses. Others claim the US government deliberately allowed the plane to be shot down to gain a propaganda advantage against the Soviet Union during the Cold War. These theories often cite inconsistencies in official reports, missing flight recorder data, and the perceived lack of a full and transparent investigation. Mainstream investigations, including those by the ICAO and various national governments, attribute the tragedy to navigational errors by the flight crew, leading the plane significantly off course and into Soviet airspace. They dismiss the conspiracy theories as lacking credible evidence, emphasizing the political climate of the Cold War and the Soviet Union's sensitivity to perceived incursions into its territory. However, the lack of complete transparency and the ongoing geopolitical tensions continue to fuel speculation and distrust, keeping the conspiracy theories alive in some segments of Korean society.
subtitle: A Cold War Tragedy and Enduring Theories

claims.claim: KAL007 was on a secret intelligence mission.
claims.evidence: Deviations from flight path, claims of electronic warfare capabilities on board, alleged communication intercepts.
claims.criticism: Flight path deviations attributed to pilot error; no credible evidence of intelligence equipment; communication intercepts unverified.

claims.claim: The US government deliberately allowed the plane to be shot down.
claims.evidence: Claims of US knowledge of the plane's deviation and inaction, alleged manipulation of radar data, desire to escalate Cold War tensions.
claims.criticism: Lack of evidence of prior US knowledge; radar data explanations exist; escalation theory lacks clear motive and supporting evidence.

claims.claim: Key evidence, such as flight recorders, has been tampered with or suppressed.
claims.evidence: Inconsistencies in official reports, claims of altered data, lack of full transparency in investigations.
claims.criticism: Flight recorder analysis released; inconsistencies explained as translation errors or interpretation differences; investigations deemed thorough by many international bodies.

keyFigures.name: Bert Schlossberg
keyFigures.role: Aviation historian and conspiracy theorist who has written extensively on the incident.

keyFigures.name: Oliver Clubb
keyFigures.role: American diplomat and historian who criticized the official US account of the incident.

keyFigures.name: Alexander Dallin
keyFigures.role: Historian who chaired the ICAO investigation into the incident, concluding pilot error as the primary cause.

mythologyConnections: None direct, but touches on themes of national trauma and distrust of powerful nations, similar to historical grievances.

timeline.event: KAL007 is shot down by Soviet fighter jets.
ICAO begins its investigation into the incident.
Russian government releases the flight recorder data to ICAO.
Conspiracy theories continue to circulate, particularly in South Korea.

sources.author: ICAO
sources.work: ICAO Council Action on the Korean Air Lines Incident of 31 August 1983
sources.title: ICAO

sources.author: Pearson, Tristan

sources.title: Pearson, Tristan
